channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: Letting go of the reins
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

Reflective Practice is an oft-used term nowadays, particularly in the field of education.

How does this manifest in the classroom?

In a school?

In the principal’s office?

This is a column that will describe true episodes that capture this, supported by links to videos for the more interested reader.

For several years, a school principal had been sending some of her teachers to attend workshops or conferences. Every now and then, she would scan announcements on social media and in her educational circles to select themes that resonated with (her perceived) needs of the faculty. She would then diligently follow up with the attendees to ensure that they returned and shared their new learnings with the rest of the teachers.

But she now realized that she was dissatisfied with the outcome.

Not much change percolated down to the faculty – and even if it did, it quickly trickled away so that everyone inevitably returned to the humdrum routine.

She began to wonder if she could move away from this ‘top-down model’ of teacher development, and instead have her teachers take ownership of their own development.

“After all, isn’t that what we wish for our students?” she mused. “And unless teachers actually experience taking onus of their own capacity building, how are they going to bring about such autonomy in their students?”

With this laudable intent, she set about sharing her thoughts with other members of a Learning Community that she was part of. She also started reading papers and thinking about all that she had absorbed.

And this is when deep reflection was triggered.

One statement stood out from a paper that she had read: teachers should be respected to come up with their own articulation of their specific learning needs. While she kept this statement in the background, the initial idea that popped into her head was that of travelling the traditional route – viz., plotting a career graph of her teachers, so as to map their progress. In addition, she wanted to hear their own vision of their ‘dream school’.

But the more she thought about these two aspects, the more she wondered why she should plot career graphs of teachers. On what basis would she judge them? After all, she had interviewed them and recruited them with some rational basis for their selection. If they were appearing to be inadequate in some areas, was it not her responsibility now to assist them in filling these gaps? How would a career graph help here?

As for vision, the school already had a Vision Statement. Any school should align itself with its articulated Vision Statement. Instead of having each teacher articulate their own vision, wouldn’t it make more sense to get teachers to brainstorm on how they could collectively help realize the school’s Vision Statement?

This simple reflection caused a significant shift in her own thinking.

She dropped her initial plan of a career graph and articulation of individual vision statements by teachers. Instead, she began listening to the feedback from members of her Learning Community with a more open mind.

Pretty soon, the doors opened to a new approach to Teacher Professional Development. She started by suggesting summer reading to the teachers, and chose books that aligned with the National Educational Policy. Just as her own reflection caused a shift in her thinking, she now wanted teachers to experience it. So, she designed a questionnaire that would draw out their thoughts, their areas of discontent and the areas that (they felt) needed to be changed, so as to realize the vision of the school.

This set in motion the wheels of a teacher-driven Teacher Professional Development Programme. Each teacher identified their own areas of development and she slowly and consistently facilitated their way forward. She periodically experienced catching herself when she slipped into a habitual ‘telling’ teachers what to do, instead of ‘letting’ them discover their own ways and forge their paths ahead. (Isn’t this akin to a teacher holding back from telling students ‘the right answer’ and allowing them instead to think it through for themselves?!)

And develop a novel Teacher Development Programme she did!

If you wish to know how this panned out, visit: https://youtu.be/ceOz_lrKIOk and https://youtu.be/ZdSOElkvPGw.

The author is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
According to Neeraja Raghavan, shifting from a top-down model to a teacher-driven approach, fosters teachers’ ownership and reflection in professional development.

item.title: Revisiting a premise
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

As a teacher, how often have you felt that you were knocking your head against a wall?

That no matter how hard you try, this student is simply not getting it?

And have you given up sometimes, realizing that you are human and can’t possibly reach out to every single child?

If you have, then this account will resonate with you.

I recently spoke to a teacher with four decades of experience and was amazed at her description of the turning point in her teaching journey. It is a story with a familiar beginning: of a lady who slipped into the profession by chance and not choice.

Somewhere along the way, she came across a student who posed a huge challenge: he just didn’t seem to absorb anything that she taught him. Try as she might, she couldn’t succeed in getting across to the boy. He failed his end-of-year exams twice.

Frustrated with her persistent efforts falling flat each time, she went with the problem to her principal. At that time, the school policy allowed a teacher to request that the school dismiss a student who failed twice in the same grade. (When she spoke to me, however, this was no longer true.)

So this despairing teacher made a sound case before her principal to dismiss this ‘good-for-nothing’ boy, and answered every question posed by the principal with convincing answers to support her case. “Surely he was good at something?” insisted the principal. No, he wasn’t, asserted this teacher firmly.

And so the errant boy was indeed dismissed.

The academic year ended, and the teacher resumed her teaching duties the next year. Seeing her batch of promoted students, she noticed the absence of that boy. Upon enquiring about his whereabouts, she received the shocking reply: “He is working in a cycle repair shop.”

That hit her like a ton of bricks.

Slowly, she came to know that he was the son of a gardener, who (what is worse) was a drunkard.

And she had ensured that such a boy never received a school education! Worse, she had even felt proud of that move!

This was the turning point in her life.

She switched instantly from being a teacher to a researcher.

What was the cause of a child not performing well?

Was it always sheer indifference?

Could there be other reasons?

With an undying thirst, this teacher began to learn about learning disabilities and methods of teaching children who faced difficulty. This was in the era before the internet, so there was no instant click of a button to help her. Her hunger to learn was the ‘mouse’ that she kept clicking. It was also the period when an awareness of children with special needs was low: unlike today.

As she began examining the specific difficulties that children faced, her paths crossed specialists in NIMHANS, Bengaluru, resource centres and workshops that she scoured the daily newspapers for announcements of. This led to her forming a section in her school for children with specific learning needs, where she and her equally enthusiastic colleague designed tailor-made lessons for their students.

As she developed customized methods to tackle specific learning difficulties of students, that boy she had been instrumental in dismissing kept popping up in her mind. And guess what she did? She went on a search: for that student! Not only did she find him two years later, but he was re-enrolled in her school and she succeeded in helping him pass the class X board exam!

Not surprisingly, this teacher won the NCERT Award for being an innovative teacher: but more than any award, her unique retracing of steps to correct a biased premise is priceless indeed.

What could well have continued as a mechanical emphasis on performance alone, heedless of underlying causes for failure, made this teacher go out of her way to reflect upon (and understand) the root cause of such difficulties.

Watch her detailed narration of her journey here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=thW_fh5sSQ0&t=9s

The author is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
What made a teacher with decades of experience rethink her approach to teaching? Neeraja Raghavan shares an inspiring story.

item.title: Seeing ‘Missed Takes’ in mistakes
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

“There is no room for mistakes in my classroom!” These words from her own school teacher have remained in the mind of an experienced teacher of English (and a Head of department today) in a leading school in Hyderabad.

Accustomed to a culture that condemned mistakes and upheld the practice of never committing any error, this teacher (when she turned into one herself) made a few discoveries at an advanced stage of her teaching career: and it all happened through a course that she enrolled in.

Titled SEEING MISSED TAKES IN MISTAKES, she affirmed that this course helped her get into the minds of her students. How did this happen? She found that her students began articulating their thought processes while committing errors, as they started examining the errors that they had made. Even though her years of experience as a teacher had inevitably included correcting students’ mistakes – and even discussing them occasionally – she candidly admitted that until she took this course, she had never really regarded errors as resources: which first required setting the ground for an honest and comfortable discussion of errors.

“It is not an easy task to own up to mistakes! I still remember my English teacher’s dictum, after all!” She laughingly confessed. And then, she added profoundly: “If we condemn making mistakes, students will begin to condemn learning.”

So how did this teacher go about her revelatory process? Firstly, she viewed mistakes as a very natural part of the learning process, instead of condemning them. Beginning with an honest admission of the mistakes that she would make when she was a student herself – and how she had often frustrated her own teachers with her repetitive patterns of errors – she found that hesitant students slowly began to open up and confess their own error patterns, with less and less shame or hesitation.

Gradually, she worked with another teacher in her English department to share her own learnings from the course. Then, she even went so far as to encourage that teacher to put into practice some of the strategies that she had acquired through the course.

So did this demand too much of that teacher’s time? Surprisingly, not for too long, she confessed. Starting with crafting dummy worksheets, where she deliberately made the same types of errors that her students usually made, she then got her students to check these worksheets – which she claimed she had sourced from a similar grade of another school.

Naturally, as they were now sitting in the chair of a teacher, her fourth graders had great fun.

“It was a laughter riot,” declared the teacher, “nonetheless, with a lot of learning!”

They began exclaiming:

“Even I make these errors, ma’am!”

“These students are not even proof-reading their work before turning it in!”

All this happened within one 40-minute period. Now that they had freely examined another’s errors, they were more open to revisiting their own – with an open mind.

“Can you sit with me for five minutes, ma’am? I keep making mistakes with the use of articles…please show me how to get over this!”

This, declared the teacher, was a paradigm shift: when students began to open their minds to metacognition. “Why did I think thus? How can I revisit my own thinking?”

The focus then shifted from merely distributing remedial worksheets to asking why certain errors are made – and often repeatedly. Not surprisingly, student performance improved significantly within a short timeframe of five months: starting from noticeably better handwriting and timely submission of work, to actual drop in the number of repetitive errors.

She sums up her own shift thus: “Today, I see myself as a teacher-educator who welcomes mistakes in my class. Because, for me, I see the need to move beyond right and wrong and use mistakes as a resource to probe thought processes.”

Watch her video here: https://youtu.be/vykX9f45XpI?si=Ew4f17jX3BecABtX

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
What works better: correcting mistakes or learning from them? Neeraja Raghavan writes about how a simple mindset shift turned mistakes into stepping stones for deeper understanding.

item.title: A teacher’s approach
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

Have you ever noticed where your central focus is when you teach? Have your expectations ever affected the way things unfold?

You may understand what I mean if you hear this true story of a young yoga teacher in a residential school in South India.

Certified (and therefore hired) as a yoga teacher, she also donned two other roles: that of a houseparent and a volunteer for Kalaripayattu[*]. An expert would come every month from Kerala to teach Kalaripayattu, and this yoga teacher oversaw the practice of this martial art whenever the expert returned to Kerala. Why did she volunteer for this? Well, she had learnt karate, and Kalaripayattu is the most ancient marital art – in fact, it is considered to be the mother of all other forms of marital art. So her innate desire to learn this art form prompted her to volunteer to oversee students’ practice. Of course, she concurrently attended to her other duties: that of a houseparent as well as teaching yoga.

Pretty soon, she noticed a stark difference between the impact of yoga and Kalaripayattu. In the practice sessions of the latter, students wouldn’t mind staying on for hours, and would even be reluctant to leave the hall when practice ended. Whereas, when it came to yoga (the domain of her expertise), their lack of eagerness would be palpable in their body language as they ambled into her yoga classes. Students would often miss class or make up excuses to come late/leave early.

Why was this happening? It took her two long years to find the answer.

“I had entered Kalaripayattu classes out of my own volition. I did not bear the tag of an ‘expert’ nor did I hold rigid expectations. I just flowed along and enjoyed the practice sessions with the children. I too was learning along with them,” she declared. “But as a yoga teacher, I felt the need to control the way things went… to prove myself. I had certain set expectations: I thought I knew how things should proceed. You see, I was focusing more on myself in yoga than on the students!”

It was small wonder, then, that the students enjoyed Kalaripayattu more! No sooner did this realization hit than she grabbed the first opportunity to probe the students’ minds! In a substitution period, she asked them what they wanted from yoga. [“I had never done this before, you see! Because I thought I knew what they wanted!” she candidly confessed.]

To her surprise, they came out with very mature responses. Some wanted enhanced immunity, while others wanted to be more spiritual. Slowly, she began tailoring her yoga sessions so as to align them with the students’ expectations, and thus began a turnaround. Students began showing up for yoga even on exam days (when school rules allowed them to miss yoga!) They enthusiastically worked towards goals (like 36 Suryanamaskaras in a certain period of time) and what’s more, showed visible enjoyment.

Her obsessive concern with results and the need to prove herself slowly melted away as she slipped into enjoying yoga practice.

“Whenever I conduct a yoga class, nowadays, I remind myself that it is not about me, the teacher. It is for the students and they are my primary focus!”

As she began letting go of rigid expectations, naturally, so did her students. The buds of enjoyment began to blossom.

Ironically, she thus learnt the true meaning of yoga as taught by Sri Krishna in the Bhagavad Gita:

To action alone you have the right, and not to the fruits. Do not be impelled by the fruits of work. Nor have attachment to inaction. – Bhagavad Gita 2.47

Watch the video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LRPGaz8QpfU&t=14s

[*]Kalaripayattu is the oldest martial art in India – from Kerala – with a history of over 3,000 years.

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
A young yoga teacher learns how centering her teaching on students, instead of herself, started a transformative journey that helped her students rediscover the joy of learning.

item.title: Walking the talk
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

It was time for annual stocktaking in school. The principal oversaw this routine every year, and as she briskly went around checking if all items were being examined, she paused at the school library. Piles of unused books were being dusted off from the shelves and scrutinized against the booklist.

“I didn’t realize we had so many books!” she exclaimed. “We seem to look at them only during this annual activity! Why don’t they get read more often?”

That question stayed with her even as she ran through her more-than-usually packed day at the end of the academic year.

Weren’t books meant to be read? What could be done about this? How could she make reading an integral part of the school curriculum?

Slowly, she began to think of ways to create a reading culture among her students, at least the 8-12 year-olds to begin with.

I happened to speak to the principal as she started planning her action research on the following problem: To develop strong reading habits (as part of the curriculum) in 8-12 year-olds.

Almost as soon as we began to chat, she realized that she would not be in a position to conduct the action research (AR) on her own, bogged down as she was by her administrative duties. There simply wouldn’t be enough time, she acknowledged. How could she therefore enthuse a core group of (about eight) teachers to undertake this action research? To do that, she would have to first infuse her teachers with a love for reading. Not that there weren’t avid readers in her teaching faculty – there were a few. But by and large, her teachers restricted themselves to reading the textbooks so as to transact the lessons in class.

Then came the question: How would her engagement with the teachers differ from that with students, since she would have done the latter if she conducted AR for the stated problem?

Now she realized that she would have to reframe the AR problem thus: To enthuse teachers to cultivate a reading culture in 8-12 year-old students in the school.

As we got deeper into the discussion, I casually asked her about her own reading habits.

“Were you always a good reader, from childhood?”

“I don’t read much,” she admitted. “I read newspaper articles, and whatever is required for a given task. Easy reading I can always do. But I don’t read anything apart from that. No books …”

I then asked her why this was so. Was it lack of access? Lack of time? She honestly admitted that she had never really developed the habit herself. “I am unable to complete whatever I start reading…I buy lots of books, but somehow I never get around to finish reading what I start …”

This made both of us sit up. Surely if she could get to the root of her own inner resistance to reading regularly, she would be better equipped to conduct this sort of AR?

The principal saw how much her AR problem had shifted. It now seemed far more feasible. And this got her very excited. She began devoting an hour a day to reading – from the very next day.

The best part of this story is that we had this conversation more than three years ago.

I last spoke to her a week ago: and would you believe it, she has not missed a single day since! She has continued the practice of devoting an hour every day to reading ever since that day; in short, she has formed a new habit in her middle age!

We often stress the importance of a teacher/parent modelling the behaviour that they wish to see in their students/children.

If a principal models the behaviour that she wishes to see in her teachers, aren’t the chances of a ripple effect in the entire school far higher?

Watch the video here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CUdphAdOVhA&t=50s

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
What began as a routine library check led to a powerful personal transformation for a school principal writes Neeraja Raghavan.

item.title: Writing as a stepping stone
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

This is the story of two teachers from two different places: a fifth grade Hindi teacher and a second grade EVS teacher – and how they came together in a unique way.

Both belong to an online community of teachers that has been meeting regularly for over four years now, and have even carried out action research on several topics. They ascribe much of their vibrancy and brimming ideas to this enriching community.

As they read research papers for their action research, one paper particularly caught the attention of the EVS teacher as it proposed ways in which students could write about real-life situations.

She shared this idea excitedly with her second graders, but was surprised by the response of one seven-year-old girl: “Why should I write when there is so much technology today to do it for me?” This was familiar to the Hindi teacher too, as she had experienced a good deal of resistance from her students to write in Hindi, try as she might to infuse the writing with zing – like having them write to the district magistrate or the prime minister, etc.

“Children just don’t want to write these days!” both teachers bemoaned.

The urge to develop their students’ writing skills was germinating within both teachers. Since the Hindi teacher was teaching the fifth grade and the EVS teacher was now being asked to handle the fourth and fifth grades, both of them brainstormed on ways to get their students to cultivate the habit of writing. It almost seemed like three pieces of a jigsaw puzzle fit snugly into place: the idea from the research paper, their friendship, and the need to get their students to write.

Like a 1000 watt bulb, an idea flashed in both their minds: “Why not get their students to write letters to each other? Across the two locations?”

And this set the ball rolling – into a cascade of events that had a totally unexpected culmination! Starting with an excited telephone call late at night, the two collaborators began creating a novel experience both for themselves as well as their students. Their description of the way children learnt to write meaningfully deserves to be heard (the link to the video is at the end of this article). One school used the opportunity to show how writing can be crafted and edited to fine tune it, while the other kept the original tone and language of the students by keeping editing out of the plan.

As children of one school began to get to know their friends from the other school, they felt like meeting them physically. What started off as kindling the love for writing in children (and helping them see its value even in this age of AI) now turned into something larger: children could get exposed to their counterparts in a different state, learn about the ways of that school and community and forge deeper links with their pen friends, if they met in person. Pretty soon, the children owned the idea of visiting the other school, and became the advocates of the plan as they convinced their parents. So too did other teachers in the two schools: as they got infected by the enthusiasm of the protagonists, and soon saw value in this initiative. The exchange of letters had kindled the curiosity of the children about different personalities, and this could only be quenched when they met in person.

Both teachers now had to work hard to convince their respective school managements of the value of a visit to the other school. Donning the role of administrators was new to both, but their drive to see this happen propelled them. Naturally, since this required other teachers of their respective schools to pitch in, this duo worked systematically to break the program into steps, so as to avoid overwhelming other teachers who had not participated at the ideation stage. I was struck by their cognizance of dropping the assumption that everyone would share their enthusiasm about their idea.

Who would have thought that an intent to develop writing skills in children would end up like this?

Click on the link below to know how the exchange program flowered https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uFuZLsd-G4g

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
Few things are required to make writing exciting again: One simple idea and two passionate teachers. Read Neeraja Raghavan’s story on pen pals and possibilities.

item.title: Transforming a reluctant reader in six days
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

Have you struggled to get your child/student to read? Especially in today’s WhatsApp era, where everything that needs to be said is to be done in as few words as possible?

If you have, you might enjoy this true story. Better still, you may find that this anecdote can initiate a brainstorming session in your own school.

If you can use this episode in some way in your own working environment, then the purpose of this column will be fulfilled!

It was during the COVID pandemic that the principal of a school noticed something on her virtual rounds. One boy was consistent in his resistance to read: he always claimed to have an internet connection with very poor bandwidth. [He didn’t seem to have a problem with his internet when he was not called out to read, however.]

The principal spoke to the boy concerned and requested him to send her an audio recording daily by 5 pm, of him reading a short extract aloud. Barring an initial tardiness, the recordings began dropping into her WhatsApp daily and voila! within six days, she noticed a significant improvement in his vocabulary as well as his self-confidence. When the principal shared the read-aloud recording with the boy’s English teacher, asking her to guess who this boy was, the teacher was unable to do so!

Clearly, we teachers miss the hidden potential of some of our students – human as we are!

The Principal then reflected on the root cause for this speedy success.

In a half hour clip (please see the link at the end of this article), she describes the nature of her instructional intervention. Aimed at a specific change, an instructional intervention can be formalized – and it will often demand follow-up. In this case, the principal systematically followed up with the student for about a week, and was very satisfied with the results.

There is more to this story. When this principal shared her experience with her colleagues who were all part of an online group, they reflected collectively on the possible reasons for her success. The following questions were discussed:

[In order to get a full picture, you will need to watch this clip. I am capturing the essence of the discussion here.]

How did this transformation happen so quickly? The principal shared how she often conveys to students that they need to alter their attitude – if she feels that is needed. What enabled the principal to get such a simple idea and why didn’t it strike the teacher concerned? When – if at all – does a harried teacher get ideas like the principal got, to tackle such a problem? The importance of loosening one’s grip on a problem and brainstorming with colleagues emerged during this online discussion. As for the time that a teacher (imagines she) needs to invest with students individually, the group reflected on whether or not a huge investment of time was truly needed.

Further, during the online class, as the boy’s father passed by, he heard his son’s name being mentioned several times. This triggered his interest, and quickly drew him into the practice of getting his son to read with interest. This elicited the question from the group: Do we always need to involve parents in such interventions? What if the parents are themselves not literate and the child is a first-generation learner? Will such support even be possible then?

The online discussion concluded with a description of how Helen Keller learnt her first abstract word: and remember, abstract words are hard enough for a child with all faculties to master! Helen Keller was a blind, deaf, and mute child: the account of how she learnt abstract words capped the discussion and set the teachers thinking.

Do write in and let us know if this month’s story set you thinking, too!

Watch the half-hour clip here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hftOrj4abOs&t=62s

Neeraja Raghavan is founder-director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
A principal helped her student, a reluctant reader, speak confidently in six days! Neeraja Raghavan writes about the power of thoughtful interventions.

item.title: Single most important learning
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

In his Ted Talk Alan Watkins asks this question: “If you could teach yourself, your children, anybody – just ONE thing, what would that one thing be?”

In an era that is overflowing with information – often available at a single click – I found this to be a powerful question indeed. As teachers, we are most often weighed down by heavy syllabi and packed timetables. Sifting through this morass for ONE most important learning seems impossible – or does it?

An online community of teachers didn’t think it was impossible. In fact, they found it worthwhile to ruminate on. In the three-minute clip (link at the end of this article), each one articulated what they regarded as the single most important learning.

The need for self-reflection was seen as the topmost priority for one teacher. I was struck by the fact that she emphasized ‘the need’ rather than just ‘self-reflection’. For, unless one is convinced of the need for such a practice, why would one indulge in it regularly? Of course, that led me to wonder exactly how she would get her students to experience the criticality of this need …. Another teacher named a related trait, viz., self-regulation as her single most important learning.

Adaptability was seen as crucial by another teacher: the importance of remaining pliable to the ever-changing demands of the world. I could see the importance of this, given the fast pace at which everything changes in today’s world. “To pause and observe little things,” declared another teacher. This made me stop in my tracks! I immediately paused and asked myself: “How often do I do that?” I concluded that I hadn’t really learnt to do this.

In fact, this led me to ask: How could adaptability be reconciled with pausing to notice little things? (Could it?)

To live in harmony with the world was seen as the next teacher’s single most important lesson. Quite a tall order! Could this really be called a SINGLE most important learning? Surely this must encompass numerous learnings under such a broad umbrella!

I am sure many readers resonate with the next two: Getting her students to simply enjoy the process of learning was seen as the most important thing for another teacher. Awakening a love for reading was yet another teacher’s single most important learning for her students. Who wouldn’t agree with these two?

Listening skills were highlighted by a teacher as being her most important teaching. Simple as this sounds, haven’t we all experienced not being listened to? What does it take to listen to another with full attention? And how often do we do it ourselves?

Compassion for oneself and others was deemed a very important learning by one teacher, while another stressed the importance of empathy. So also did a teacher value the trait of trusting oneself and others.

Working with one’s hands was regarded by one teacher as being her most important lesson. I couldn’t help wondering: (how) can this element be brought into every single subject?

Being mindful in the here and now was a teacher’s high-priority lesson. Gratitude was topmost on another teacher’s checklist.

Respecting multiple viewpoints was raised as a very important learning by a teacher.

All of this left me asking: if these are teachers’ single most important learnings, when (if ever) are we going to get around to teaching these things? WHY aren’t we doing so already? Clearly, teachers are not at liberty to teach what they deem to be most important, and instead, are compelled to follow the diktats of policy makers and textbook writers.

If you watch this clip here: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=x2EwHL9hn3s

Perhaps you will begin articulating your single most important learning too?

And then, maybe, even draw up your road map for actually bringing that lesson into your classroom?

Neeraja Raghavan is Founder-Director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
What is the single most important learning you would want to teach yourself, your children, or your students? Neeraja Raghavan shares how different teachers responded to this question.

item.title: Getting better at what we do
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

This month, I wish to share my own reflection on the question:

“How can a teacher keep getting better at her work?”

And the emphasis is on the phrase “keep getting”.

Yes, an occasional course or a workshop often injects us with new zeal, ideas, or even fresh content, and fuels our engines – for a while. But what then? How does one continue to cruise along with constant alertness to one’s own learning needs?

I stumbled upon a Ted talk which asked exactly this question.

One day, Dr Atul Gawande (a Boston-based American surgeon) realized that after a slow improvement in his medical practice for some years, he had plateaued. He wondered: Was there any way in which his practice could continue to improve? Those five years of medical school: were they sufficient to ensure that a doctor improved continuously for the rest of their career?

Of course not! Reflecting thus, he began to take a look at other professions. Sportsmen – even the very best – ALWAYS had a coach. Or did they? He discovered that coaching didn’t start until 1875 – when Yale hired a coach. In all Yale-Harvard football matches for the next three decades, Yale consistently won each and every match – except three! The result? Harvard hired a coach!

Was coaching restricted to sports, then, he wondered?

The violinist Itzhak Perelman declared that he definitely had a coach: his wife! She had studied music with him, and then chosen to give up her profession (as a violinist) in order to support her husband. She did this by watching each of his performances and giving him constructive feedback thereafter.

Could he, Dr Gawande, get a coach? He approached his former professor, now retired, with that request. Prof. Bob Osteen agreed. So in Dr Gawande’s next surgery, Bob Osteen was also present in the OT, quietly taking notes. After a very smooth surgery, Dr Gawande wasn’t expecting any earth-shattering feedback. But Bob shared “little things” like:

  • The light had swung out of the wound during the case.
    • Your elbow goes up in the air once in a while, instead of resting comfortably.

“Little things”? Definitely not! Especially for something as critical as surgery!

Clearly, his coach was of enormous help in:

  • Drawing his attention to “little things”.
  • Becoming his external eyes and ears.
  • Breaking down the fundamentals.
  • Providing a more accurate picture of HIS reality.
  • Coming up with a checklist.

This reinforced Dr G’s resolve to keep getting coached – and that set his learning curve on the rise again.

I now asked: If a doctor finds five years of medical school insufficient to ensure continuous improvement, how can a one-or-two-year B Ed for teachers ensure that their practice keeps getting better?

Was there a consistent coaching practice for teachers anywhere in the world?

My search led me to the age-old practice of Lesson Study in Japan. In this very interesting video of a grade II math teacher, Lesson Study is shown as consisting of these steps:

  1. The grade II math teacher sat with a group of colleagues and planned his lesson.
  2. Then, he transacted it – with his colleagues observing the class and taking notes.
  3. All of them then collectively analyzed the lesson.
  4. He incorporated their feedback and refined this lesson for future transactions.

Do watch this clip, as it will help you gain a better understanding.

My takeaways are:

  • Teachers who wish to continuously improve can do so through the process of Lesson Study.
  • For this, they need to find trusted educators who will observe their classroom transactions and offer constructive feedback.

And the cherry on the cake? A community of practice slowly builds!

Any takers?

References

  1. Caroline Chassels and Wayne Melville (2009) Canadian Journal of Education Vol. 32, No. 4 pp. 734-763.
  2. Brian Doig and Susie Groves (2011) Mathematics Teacher Education & Development Vol. 13.1 pp. 77-93.

Neeraja Raghavan is Founder-Director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. The author can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
How can teachers keep improving beyond initial training? Neeraja Raghavan draws lessons from Dr. Gawande and Japan’s Lesson Study to show the power of coaching and feedback.

item.title: The courage to disagree
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan

BOOM! There was a loud explosion from the ground floor.

The new principal (just two weeks into her job) of Sri Kumaran Children’s Home, (Bengaluru) Nandashree Natarajan, hurried out of her office (on the same floor) towards the location of the explosion.

Expecting to see teachers crowding in the corridors, she braced herself to remain calm, even as she raced. To her astonishment, the corridors were empty. She was joined by just one other teacher, who had left his class on the third floor to investigate the noise.

The chemistry laboratory assistant and her helpers were safe but shaken, after they had (in their ignorance) poured water over mixed residual chemicals in the disposal area, resulting in a violent reaction.

Walking back to her office, the principal was relieved – Thank God it was a minor accident and nobody was hurt in the process!

But why had no other teacher come out to see what was wrong? It was too loud a sound not to be heard on all floors of the building. Something was holding them back – what was it?

Slowly, she began to see that the accident had nailed the burning issue: most of the faculty were in a compliant mode, doing their work sincerely and exactly as they were told to do. It was their job, their workplace, and they strove to meet expectations. As they went about their daily chores, there was a palpable lack of a sense of belonging or even a smile. Had they been told to leave their classes to investigate the cause of the explosion, they would have dutifully done so. They were busy covering the syllabus and teaching their classes as always: and doing it excellently.

The principal yearned to bring in a more open culture: where there was a sense of camaraderie among the teachers and herself, where opinions were fearlessly voiced and everyone felt that this was THEIR school.

How was she to set about triggering that change? She decided to turn this into an Action Research (AR) Problem.

In order to frame the problem, she roped in a few teachers who she had already identified as being more communicative. With their help, she systematically surveyed the entire faculty’s needs: and the findings were startling. Almost all of them articulated the need for free and open communication!

So, the AR problem became: To cultivate a work environment grounded in mutual respect, open communication, and shared values.

Next, her team designed a set of activities for five days of empowerment during the Teachers’ Correction-Time (3 to 4 pm): Motivational Monday, Trust Tuesday, Whale Done Wednesday, Thought-Provoking Thursday, and Festive Friday.

On the first Monday, this team held a motivational session to draw out teachers’ gratitude towards any colleague who had helped them in the past. For the first time, teachers openly articulated appreciation of their colleagues – which many found very moving. On Trust Tuesday, sharing of personal stories between random pairs of teachers was intended to build openness and trust.

It was after this that the penny dropped. For, just before Whale Done Wednesday, teachers expressed their reluctance to participate further – citing workload and time crunch.

The principal paused: could resistance turn into collaboration? Instead of confronting her teachers, she solicited their suggestions and feedback. This was the turning point in her AR: for excellent suggestions emerged, e.g. discussion of real-life case studies. Her team immediately crafted meaningful case studies that led to rich discussions on the following Wednesday.

Now that the ball had been set rolling, Thought-Provoking Thursday didn’t need much designing by the core team, as a Grade I teacher volunteered to make a presentation! It all culminated in a truly Festive Friday where visibly smiling teachers showcased their talents.

Happy ending? NO! “This is just the beginning of an ongoing effort to sustain bonds between my teachers and to keep communication alive,” admitted Nandashree. “While I can see less stiffness and far more smiles, I know that I have to work towards being part of a team, rather than an isolated head of a school.”

That ‘setback’ on Tuesday was in fact an advancement: for teachers who had heretofore been quietly compliant now trusted their principal enough to openly disagree!

So here is a touchstone for heads of schools/teachers:

  • How often do your teachers openly disagree with you?
  • How freely do your students express opposing views?

References

1. Whale DoneThe Power of Positive Relationships By Ken Blanchard

2. https://www.facebook.com/reel/1351022283229308

Neeraja Raghavan is Founder-Director of Thinking Teacher (www.thinkingteacher.in), an organization that networks with teachers across the country. Thinking Teacher aims to awaken and nurture the reflective practitioner within each teacher. Thinking Teacher’s goal is to help build deep inquiry and rich learning into the teaching process. She can be reached at neeraja@thinkingteacher.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neeraja Raghavan
When no teacher or student steps out of class after an accident, the principal is forced to look within. Neeraja Raghavan writes about what it really takes to build an open school culture.
